title: Pro Wrestling Goes to the Movies, Plus Stephen Merchant on ‘Fighting With My Family’ | Interview (Ep. 130)
format: Sean and David Shoemaker survey wrestling movies good and bad, then Stephen Merchant explains how he turned a WWE documentary into 'Fighting With My Family.'

films.title: The Wrestler
films.note: Darren Aronofsky's film, David's pick for the best wrestling movie ever made. Not to be confused with the 1974 movie of the same name starring Verne Gagne, which comes up only in passing.

films.title: Nacho Libre
films.note: What David would actually show his kids to explain wrestling, even though it wouldn't crack his list.

films.title: No Holds Barred
films.note: Hulk Hogan; David calls it "cocaine riddled and insane" in the best way.

films.title: Rocky III
films.note: Debated as a wrestling movie on the strength of Hogan's Thunderlips cameo.

films.title: Ready to Rumble
films.note: The cautionary example for what happens when a movie tries to partner with a real promotion, also tangled up with David Arquette's actual WCW title reign.

films.title: Body Slam
films.note: Roddy Piper and Dirk Benedict; "the classic of the genre" to David, playing it dumb but unimpeachably sincere.

films.title: Paradise Alley
films.note: Stallone's directorial debut, made right after Rocky, set in a fictionalized 1940s Lower East Side wrestling scene. Terry Funk appears.

films.title: The Foul King
films.note: A South Korean film David says nails the fantasy of becoming a wrestler and then having to actually wrestle for real.

films.title: Goldface, the Fantastic Superman
films.note: One of the odder finds David dug up researching wrestling movies.

films.title: The Big Show-Off
films.note: Arthur Lake and Dale Evans; a curiosity from the era.

films.title: Night and the City
films.note: Jules Dassin's noir, made between The Naked City and Rififi, built around one of the best wrestling scenes in any movie, with Richard Widmark, Gene Tierney, and Stanislaus Zbyszko.

films.title: Beyond the Mat
films.note: The one that hit David hardest as a young hardcore WWE fan, nearing its 20th anniversary.

films.title: Fake It So Real
films.note: Robert Greene's documentary about a tiny wrestling promotion in North Carolina; David is friends with the director.

films.title: I'm from Hollywood
films.note: The Andy Kaufman documentary about his pro-wrestling feud with Jerry Lawler.

films.title: My Breakfast with Blassie
films.note: Andy Kaufman and Freddie Blassie's parody of My Dinner With Andre.

films.title: La Lutte
films.note: A Quebecois art film about the Canadian wrestling scene, made for the National Film Board and shaped by Roland Barthes's writing on wrestling.

films.title: Lipstick & Dynamite, Piss & Vinegar: The First Ladies of Wrestling
films.note: On the early days of women's wrestling; David says it's never felt more relevant than now.

films.title: Fighting With My Family
films.note: Merchant's film, adapted from a British TV documentary Dwayne Johnson sent him, about WWE wrestler Paige (born Saraya-Jade Bevis) and her family.

films.title: Tooth Fairy
films.note: The film Merchant made with Dwayne Johnson years earlier. It's how the two stayed in touch, leading Johnson to send him the documentary.

films.title: Lady Macbeth
films.note: The only performance of Florence Pugh's Merchant had seen before casting her: "a much more contained, controlled performance" than what he needed from her here.

films.title: Billy Elliot
films.note: Merchant's touchstone for a movie that makes you root for a kid chasing a niche dream you don't personally care about.

films.title: The Fighter
films.note: Merchant borrowed its trick of shooting fight scenes with real HBO boxing cameramen for his Staples Center sequence.

films.title: Rocky
films.note: Merchant's baseline for a sports movie that works on people who don't care about the sport: there's barely any fighting until the end.

films.title: Rocky II
films.note: Merchant's favorite in the series.

films.title: Creed
films.note: Cited as the reboot that got the character-first approach right again.

films.title: 8 Mile
films.note: Another underdog-performer template Merchant had in mind.

films.title: Saturday Night Fever
films.note: Merchant's point: it's remembered as a musical but is actually a tough, dark, working-class drama that just happens to have joyous dance numbers, the same tonal mix he wanted.

films.title: Batman
films.note: Tim Burton's film, brought up over the choice to make the Joker Bruce Wayne's parents' killer, a young Merchant's version of getting mad online.

films.title: A Star Is Born
films.note: Merchant's reference for staging a huge, emotional live-performance set piece, like his own one-hour shoot at a real WWE taping in the Staples Center.

films.title: Jojo Rabbit
films.note: Merchant has a small part in Taika Waititi's then-upcoming film, alongside Thomasin McKenzie.

films.title: Leave No Trace
films.note: Merchant's pick for the last great thing he's seen: Debra Granik's film with Thomasin McKenzie, whom he'd just worked with on Jojo Rabbit.
